does: This paper conducts the first systematic evaluation of jailbreak attacks against large language models across the complete deployment pipeline, including both input filtering and output content moderation stages. Rather than evaluating attacks in isolation against bare models, the authors test nearly all existing jailbreaking techniques against a realistic inference stack with additional safety mechanisms. The key empirical finding is that nearly all evaluated jailbreak methods can be detected by at least one deployed safety filter, substantially revising upward estimates of practical defense effectiveness compared to prior work that ignored moderation components. The evaluation quantifies the detection rates across multiple filter types and identifies that safety filters can reliably catch jailbreaks while revealing trade-offs between recall and precision in current systems.
connects: This work extends prior research on LLM safety and adversarial alignment by addressing a critical gap in evaluation methodology. Previous studies assessed jailbreak techniques like prompt injection, role-playing, and constraint relaxation against unfiltered models, establishing their effectiveness in triggering harmful outputs. However, this work connects to the emerging field of practical AI deployment security by recognizing that real-world LLM systems incorporate layered defenses beyond model fine-tuning. The paper builds on content moderation and input filtering literature while directly responding to inflated threat assessments from model-centric jailbreak research. It sits within the broader arms race framing of adversarial robustness, where attack and defense capabilities continuously escalate.
enables: This evaluation framework establishes a more accurate baseline for understanding genuine safety risks in deployed LLM systems, which can guide future research priorities toward realistic threat models. By identifying which jailbreak techniques evade detection and where filter trade-offs exist, the work points toward improved defense mechanisms that maintain both security and user experience. The systematic methodology enables subsequent research to develop novel jailbreaks specifically targeting the full pipeline rather than isolated components, advancing understanding of fundamental vulnerabilities. The findings also inform practical deployment strategies for balancing safety and usability, and establish evaluation standards for assessing safety claims of new LLM systems in production environments.
